dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3, en route for abdominal pain and dizziness.
Hello, ma'am, I'm Bill with the rescue squad. How can we assist you today?
I've had lower abdominal pain for about a week. It's very severe and feels like cramping I never felt before. I also feel nauseated and have diarrhea.
Is there a time when the pain goes away or lessens at all?
No, it's always there and sometimes it gets so bad I can't even move.
Could you tell me your full name for me, please?
Jennifer.
And what year were you born?
What city are we in now?
[CITY].
Good, thank you. You are alert and oriented to person, place, and time, AOX4 (alert and oriented to four).
You have an open airway and are breathing easily. I will now begin monitoring your cardiac rhythm.
You are breathing at a rate of 16 breaths per minute, with regular and unlabored effort.
When did your symptoms first begin, and was there anything unusual about them?
It started last Wednesday with cramps and diarrhea. The cramps feel debilitating.
Did you do anything out of the ordinary the day before it started?
No, everything was normal. No food poisoning or accident.
Has the nature of your pain changed since it first started?
The pain is constant. It's sharp and cramping, not like I'm in labor.
On a scale from 0 to 10 with 10 being the worst pain possible, how would you rate your pain now?
A 10. Every time it hits, I can't walk or do anything.
Can you tell me the medications you are on regularly?
Albuterol, Advair, losartan, metformin, furosemide, Nexium, ranitidine, Flonase, sertraline, Zofran, dicyclomine, and Imodium.
What diagnosed medical conditions do you have?
I have mild asthma, diabetes, high blood pressure, and high cholesterol.
First pulse reading is 89. Slightly elevated, but within normal range.
You are breathing at 16 breaths per minute-steady rhythm and depth.
Blood pressure is 154 over 71.
Blood sugar is at 149 currently.
Oxygen saturation is 95% on room air.
Initial EKG shows normal sinus rhythm. No ST-elevation or heart issues noted.
I'll be placing you on a cardiac monitor. First, we will do a 4-lead EKG-standard practice for safety. Can I proceed?
You may go ahead. I don't mind.
We've placed the 4-lead monitoring system and it's functioning normally.
Now I'll apply the 12-lead EKG to get a more detailed read. This is standard with abdominal complaints.
GCS is 15 (E4, V5, M6), you are fully alert and conscious.
Did your recent ER visit last week help reduce your symptoms at all?
No, I was given medication, but the pain has stayed at 10 and still cramps daily as badly as before.
Based on your presentation, I am initiating the abdominal pain protocol (Protocol 3-2). We'll now be preparing for transport.
Second pulse is 87. Still within acceptable range.
Breathing remains steady at 16 respirations per minute.
Second blood pressure reading is 183 over 49. We'll monitor this closely.
Second 12-lead reading had an issue with lead V1, so we'll repeat the test now.
Final EKG shows normal sinus rhythm again. No ST-elevation or heart changes noted.
Oxygen saturation has improved to 99% on room air.
Where would you like to receive treatment, Ms. Jennifer?
[COMPANY_NAME]. I'd like to go back there for reassessment.
I will now assist you to the stretcher. You ambulated steadily from the couch. Please remain steady.
You are now comfortably positioned for transport and secured with a five-point restraint.
[COMPANY_NAME] has been contacted and is expecting you.
We are now approaching [COMPANY_NAME]. Emergency department is on standby.
Ms. Jennifer is a 54-year-old female with lower abdominal pain for a week, nausea, diarrhea, and dizziness. No signs of acute distress.
Her GCS is 15, airway and breathing are normal, and she has no signs of trauma or dehydration.
Current vital signs include pulse 86, BP 174/102, respirations 16, SpO2 94%, and EKG shows normal sinus rhythm with borderline nonspecific intraventricular conduction delay.
She has a history of diabetes, hypertension, asthma, and hyperlipidemia. Current medications are as listed.
Thank you for the full report. All details have been noted.
I am really grateful for the attention and care. You all are my only hope now.
It was a pleasure, Jennifer. You are now in good care. Best wishes.
